neighborhoods.name: Black Bottom / Paradise Valley
primary_groups: African Americans from Alabama, Georgia, Tennessee, Mississippi
neighborhoods.character: Detroit's primary Black neighborhood, bounded by Gratiot, Brush, the Detroit River, and the Grand Trunk Railroad tracks. Paradise Valley was the commercial and entertainment district along Hastings Street. Despite severe overcrowding and deteriorating housing, the neighborhood developed a vibrant nightlife and business district. Demolished in the 1950s-60s for the Chrysler Freeway and Lafayette Park urban renewal.
key_institutions: Dunbar Hospital (first Black hospital in Michigan)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} reached Detroit, Black Bottom was likely the first stop — every arriving family from the South funneled into the same overcrowded blocks east of Woodward, where Hastings Street hummed with barbershops, pool halls, and the blues.

neighborhoods.name: Hamtramck / Poletown
primary_groups: Polish immigrants, later Yemeni and Bangladeshi immigrants
neighborhoods.character: An independent city entirely surrounded by Detroit, Hamtramck became overwhelmingly Polish after Dodge Main opened in 1914. Workers walked to the plant from frame houses on narrow lots. Polish was the dominant language in shops, churches, and schools well into the 1950s.
key_institutions: Our Lady Queen of Apostles Church
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If the {surname} family was Polish and came to Detroit for auto work, Hamtramck was probably home — the Dodge Main plant was walking distance, and Polish was spoken in every store on Joseph Campau Avenue.

neighborhoods.name: Corktown
primary_groups: Irish immigrants, particularly from County Cork
neighborhoods.character: Detroit's oldest surviving neighborhood, settled by Irish famine refugees in the late 1840s. Named for County Cork, Ireland. The neighborhood was working-class Irish Catholic, centered around Most Holy Trinity Church. Many residents worked on the Michigan Central Railroad or in nearby factories.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Corktown got its name honestly — if {ancestor_name} came from County Cork and landed in Detroit, this was the neighborhood, and Most Holy Trinity's baptismal registers probably hold the record.

major_employers.name: Ford Motor Company — River Rouge Complex
major_employers.wages: The famous $5/day (1914) doubled prevailing wages. By the 1940s, UAW contracts established $1.50-2.00/hr with benefits.
major_employers.working_conditions: Henry Ford's $5 day attracted workers from across the country, but came with intrusive oversight through the Ford Sociological Department, which inspected workers' homes and personal habits. The River Rouge plant was the largest industrial complex in the world, employing over 100,000 at peak. Ford hired Black workers in greater numbers than other automakers, though largely for the most dangerous foundry jobs.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Ford's $5 day was the lure that pulled {ancestor_name} to Detroit — the money was real, twice what the South or the old country paid, though Ford's inspectors might show up at the door to see if you kept a clean house.

major_employers.name: Dodge Main / Chrysler Corporation
major_employers.wages: Comparable to Ford and GM after UAW organization: $1.25-2.50/hr (1940s-1960s) with union benefits
major_employers.working_conditions: Dodge Main in Hamtramck employed up to 35,000 workers. The plant was the economic engine of Hamtramck and drew heavily from the Polish community. Assembly line speed was grueling, and the 1937 sit-down strikes at Chrysler plants paralleled the famous GM Flint strikes.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Dodge Main was Hamtramck — if {ancestor_name} lived in Hamtramck, odds are strong that someone in the household worked at the plant on Joseph Campau and the sound of the stamping presses was the neighborhood's heartbeat.

major_employers.name: General Motors — Various Detroit-area plants
major_employers.wages: After UAW contracts: among the highest industrial wages in America, $2-4/hr (1950s-1960s) with full benefits, pensions
major_employers.working_conditions: GM operated multiple plants in the Detroit area including Cadillac, Fleetwood, Fisher Body, and Ternstedt. The 1936-37 Flint sit-down strike established the UAW and transformed auto worker conditions. GM jobs became pathways to middle-class homeownership for both white ethnic and Black workers.
major_employers.narrative_hook: A General Motors paycheck meant {ancestor_name} could buy a house, send children to school, and join the middle class — the UAW contract was the great equalizer for families who had arrived with nothing.

port_of_entry_details.facility: Detroit was an inland destination. European immigrants arrived via East Coast ports (Ellis Island, Castle Garden) and transferred by rail, or crossed from Canada via the Detroit River. Southern Black migrants arrived by rail, primarily via the Louisville & Nashville and the Michigan Central Railroads.
port_of_entry_details.processing: No immigrant processing facility in Detroit. Canadian border crossing records exist at the National Archives (Detroit District). Immigrants from East Coast ports are documented in New York, Boston, Philadelphia, or Baltimore ship manifests.
records_generated: Ship manifests at port of entry
Canadian border crossing records (for those entering via Ontario)

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Boarding houses near factories, shared flats in older housing stock. In Black Bottom, single-family homes were subdivided into multiple family units. New arrivals from the South often stayed with relatives or church contacts for the first weeks.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Polish workers clustered in Hamtramck near Dodge Main. Irish settled in Corktown. Black residents were confined to Black Bottom and later expanded to the West Side. Arab immigrants (largely from Lebanon and Yemen) settled in Dearborn's South End near the Rouge plant.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Auto industry wages enabled relatively rapid homeownership — Ford's $5 day was designed partly to promote stability. Pattern: boarding house (months) to rental flat (1-3 years) to purchased bungalow (5-10 years). Black families faced restricted housing markets and paid premium rents for inferior housing.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Detroit's explosive growth created intense competition for housing and jobs. Racial tensions between Black and white workers were exploited by employers to prevent unionization, then erupted in violent confrontation.
specific_incidents: 1925 Ossian Sweet case — Black doctor's family defended home in white neighborhood, defended by Clarence Darrow
1942 Sojourner Truth housing project riot — white mobs attacked Black families moving into federal housing
1943 Detroit Race Riot — 34 dead, 675 injured, federal troops deployed
nativist_organizations: Black Legion (1930s KKK offshoot active in Detroit auto plants)
Improvement associations (white homeowner groups opposing Black neighbors)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Second Baptist Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community, Underground Railroad station, Great Migration arrivals
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism and marriage records from 1836. One of the oldest Black churches in the Midwest.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Second Baptist Church was a stop on the Underground Railroad before the Civil War and a reception center for Great Migration arrivals after — if the {surname} family joined a church on arrival, Second Baptist's membership rolls may document them.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. Florian Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death records in Polish and Latin. Parish census records. Records held by Archdiocese of Detroit.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: If the {surname} family was part of Hamtramck's Polish community, St. Florian's sacramental registers probably recorded their baptisms, marriages, and funerals — the parish was the center of Polish life in the shadow of Dodge Main.

source_references.author: Thomas J. Sugrue
source_references.title: The Origins of the Urban Crisis: Race and Inequality in Postwar Detroit
source_references.notes: Prize-winning study of Detroit's racial and economic transformation, documenting housing discrimination, deindustrialization, and neighborhood change from the 1940s forward.

source_references.author: Beth Tompkins Bates
source_references.title: The Making of Black Detroit in the Age of Henry Ford
source_references.notes: Documents the relationship between Ford Motor Company, Black workers, and the development of Detroit's Black community, including the role of churches and the NAACP.

source_references.author: Thaddeus C. Radzilowski
source_references.title: The View from a Polish Ghetto: Some Observations on the First Hundred Years in Detroit
source_references.notes: Detailed study of Polish community formation in Hamtramck and Detroit, covering parish structure, labor organizing, and cultural institutions.
